title: Blood in Dog Urine (Hematuria): Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: If your dog has blood in their urine, it is a high-priority medical concern. Learn the causes, urgency levels, and how to help your vet today.
content_html:
TL;DR: Blood in a dog's urine is a high-priority medical concern that requires a veterinary exam to rule out infections, toxins, or blockages; seek immediate emergency care if your pet cannot urinate or appears lethargic.

What is hematuria and why is there blood in my dog's urine?

Hematuria is the medical term for the presence of red blood cells in your dog's urine. It may appear as pink-tinged fluid, bright red streaks, or dark, cola-colored urine. This symptom indicates that bleeding is occurring somewhere within the urinary system, which includes the kidneys, the bladder, or the tubes connecting them. It is often accompanied by frequent attempts to urinate or signs of discomfort.

Is blood in my dog's urine a veterinary emergency?

The urgency level for blood in the urine is High. While it can be caused by treatable conditions like a urinary tract infection (UTI) or bladder stones, it can also signal life-threatening issues. You must seek emergency veterinary care immediately if your dog exhibits:

  • Straining or the inability to pass urine
  • Ingestion of rodenticide (rat poison)
  • Severe trauma
  • Lethargy
  • Pale gums

How can taking a photo of my dog's urine help with the triage process?

If your dog urinated on a light-colored surface like concrete, snow, or a puppy pad, a clear photograph provides vital information for the medical team by:

  • Helping determine the severity of the bleeding and the concentration of the urine
  • Identifying the presence of blood clots
  • Allowing the vet to distinguish between diagnostic possibilities more quickly
  • Assisting in the rapid triaging of your pet upon arrival

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Hematuria indicates hemorrhage within the urogenital system, although iatrogenic hemorrhage can occur during sample collection via cystocentesis or catheterization. In animals with bladder or urethral tumors, hematuria, dysuria, stranguria, and pollakiuria are common clinical signs. Urinalysis frequently reveals hematuria, and evidence of secondary infection may be present. A thickened bladder wall or palpable urethral mass may be detected rectally. Ureteral obstruction may cause abdominal pain and a palpable, enlarged kidney, whereas bilateral ureteral or urethral obstruction may result in signs of uremia.

Chapter: Urology, Oncology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1523)

faqs.question: Can blood in dog urine go away on its own?
faqs.answer: No. Blood in the urine is a clinical sign of an underlying problem that requires professional diagnosis and treatment. Delaying care can lead to more serious complications.

faqs.question: What if my dog is acting normal but has bloody urine?
faqs.answer: Even if your dog is eating and playing, blood in the urine indicates an internal issue like stones or an infection that needs to be addressed before it causes significant pain or becomes a crisis.

faqs.question: Should I bring a urine sample to the vet?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you can collect a fresh, clean sample in a sterile container, it can speed up the diagnostic process. However, do not delay your visit if you cannot collect one.
